measures to put it into use and a decision must be made on what is the
most beneficial to the government, otherwise relevant disbursements will
be declared unnecessary and thus will be disallowed in audit.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer and the
Municipal Accountant to ensure the timely release of the barangays’ shares from
RPT collections in compliance with Section 271(d) of RA No. 7160.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the members of the Bids and
Awards Committee (BAC) and other officials concerned to adhere to the
provisions prescribed in the 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184 regarding warranty
security, either by deducting retention money from payments or requiring a
special bank guarantee, otherwise all expenses or damages that may arise due to
defects in the goods delivered within the warranty period shall be charged to
concerned responsible officials.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
require every AO who will receive a cash advance to strictly observe the prescribed
due date for the liquidation of his/her cash advance and to ensure that all cash
advances are duly liquidated and/or refunded at the end of each year, in
compliance with COA Circular No. 97-002, to safeguard cash from the risk of
misuse or loss.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. instruct the Market Administrator to post Notices of Vacant or Notices of
Newly Constructed Stall in the prescribed areas to fully utilize the spaces
available for leasing in order to maximize the income opportunities of the
Municipality;
b. direct the Market Administrator to execute the Contracts of Lease between
the remaining 29 lessee and the Municipality in his presence and the
members of the Market Committee; and
c. ensure that judicious planning is made before undertaking new
infrastructure projects taking into consideration provisions for the
appropriate utilization of available spaces to avoid loss and/or wastage of
public fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the MDRRMO to submit to the
Office of the Auditor through the MDRRMC and Municipal Development Council
(MDC) the copy of Monthly Report of Sources and Utilization of DRRMF on or
before the 15th day after the end of each month, as prescribed under COA Circular
No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012, to facilitate the timely evaluation of the
implementation of disaster risk management activities and early communication
to Management of the noted deficiencies, if any.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct their SWMB which was
organized on September 12, 2023 to prioritize the review of the MSWP in order to
assess its sustainability, viability and effectiveness. Furthermore, we recommend
that the Municipal Mayor ensure that the MSWMB is able to perform it duties and
responsibilities as outlined in Section 12 of the Ecological Solid Waste
Management Act.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct their SWMB to establish in
each Barangay or cluster of Barangays a MRF taking into consideration the
prescribed layout/design for Department of Environment and Natural Resources
(DENR)-Environmental Management Bureau-funded MRF in order to ensure that
the facility can receive, sort, process, and store compostable and recyclable
material efficiently and in an environmentally sound manner.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and the GSO to study and consider availing of the provisions of the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances by complying with the guidelines provided under COA Circular No. 2020-006, amongst which are as follows:
Construction in Progress (CIP),
prepare and submit the PIP for approval of the Municipal Mayor and submit a copy thereof to the COA at least 10 calendar days before the scheduled start of inventory-taking activities;
prepare the consolidated RPCPPE classified according to type/category for reconciliation with the PPE records provided by the Municipal Accounting Office;
conduct disposition and de-recognition procedures for missing or valueless PPE, if any; and
conduct other procedures as may be necessary to complete and finish the one-time cleansing of PPE.
We also recommended the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to compute and record the depreciation expenses of the PPE accounts totaling P129,973,833.72 to ensure that the relevant accounts are presented fairly in the financial statements of the Municipality, in compliance with Paragraph 21 of IPSAS 17 and Section 262 of the GAM for LGUs, Volume I.
observations.managementAction:
a. For the Municipal Accountant and GSO to conduct the preliminary activities such as the updating and comparison of their respective RPCPPE and other records prior to the preparation of the PIP; b. Prepare and submit the PIP for approval of the Municipal Mayor and submit a copy thereof to the COA at least 10 calendar days before the scheduled start of inventory-taking activities; c. Prepare the consolidated RPCPPE classified according to type/category for reconciliation with the PPE records provided by the Municipal Accounting Office; d. Conduct disposition and de-recognition procedures for missing or valueless PPE, if any; and e. Conduct other procedures as may be necessary to complete and finish the one-time cleansing of PPE.
The Municipal Accountant submitted a detailed variance analysis and made adjusting Journal Entries to drop the donated vehicles to the barangay. On the other hand, the GSO also made adjustments for the cost of properties recorded in the accounting records. The Municipal Accountant computed and recorded the depreciation expenses of the Infrastructure Assets.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to increase the amount of fidelity bond of the subject AOs to sufficiently cover their cash accountabilities pursuant to Treasury Circular No. 02-2009 to protect the interest of the Municipality, otherwise, limit the amount of their cash advances up to the maximum cash accountability.
observations.managementAction: The bonds of other bonded officials are already increased to avoid creation of cash advances carrying the amount exceeding their accountability.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Municipal Treasurer to ensure that all cancelled checks are marked cancelled on their face and subsequently attach them to the RCI submitted to the COA; and
b. Municipal Accountant to submit to the COA the RCI and the cancelled checks for verification and inspection.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer ensured that all checks were marked cancelled on their face and attached to the RCI. The Treasury Office prepared and submitted the RCI monthly and is submitted by the Accounting Office to the COA.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer and other concerned officials to prepare and submit the necessary requirements to enable the Municipality to include in its automated payroll system the payment of the wages of its JO and COS workers.
observations.managementAction: From July 2023 to date, the Management has been processing the payment of wages of JO as well as contractual personnel in School through an automated payroll system.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
a. Municipal Engineer to ensure that Suspension Orders on projects are only issued based on the grounds and/or in accordance with the procedures provided in Section 10.1 of Annex “E” of the 2016 RIRR of RA 9184 and pertinent sections of the LGUs Procurement Manual; b. Municipal Engineer and the Municipal Accountant to compute the liquidated damages equivalent to the delays in the completion of the project “Repair and Improvement of Municipal Health Center” and demand from the concerned Contractor the payment thereof, otherwise the Audit Team shall issue a Notice of Disallowance; c. Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to specify in the contract the duration and/or start and target dates of completion and more importantly, the provision on the liquidated damages.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Engineer ensured that Suspension Orders on projects are only issued based on the grounds and/or in accordance with the procedures provided. The Municipal Accountant sent a demand letter to the subject contractor to urge the latter to settle the liquidated damages and the contractor already made the payment. The BAC specified in the contract the duration and the contract provision on the liquidated damages. The Municipal Accountant strictly imposed and deducted liquidated damages to the delays of the Contractor before the final payment was made.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Planning and Development Officer and Municipal Engineer to ensure the optimal and timely utilization of 20 percent DF in the succeeding period by including development projects that are well-planned and implementation-ready.
observations.managementAction: Implemented the eight projects included in the 2022 Development Fund. However, upon verification, the 20 Dialysis Center project remained non-operational.
observations.reason: However, upon verification, the 20 Dialysis Center project remained non-operational.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, the Municipal Accountant and the Head, Human Resource Management Office (HRMO) to submit the following documents to the Audit Team for evaluation, to wit; a. documents such as Personal Data Sheet (PDS), Certificates of Training or equivalent documents that will prove the competence and qualifications of the 60 workers to carry out their functions; and b. documents detailing their functions and responsibilities and actual work accomplishments, including the locations/areas of their assignments and the number of hours or days rendered during the period covered by the payrolls, duly signed by the workers concerned and verified by their immediate supervisors, otherwise all payments to them will be suspended and/or disallowed in audit.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality ensures that all of the individuals hired for the Peace and Order and Public Safety Campaign hold technical expertise and can submit proof of qualification. The Municipality will ensure that all of the individuals hired for the Peace and Order and Public Safety Campaign are capable of carrying out their duties and responsibilities and will regularly submit an actual work accomplishment report indicating all the information needed. The Municipality of the Mayor submitted the documents during the CY 2022 audit conference held on May 24, 2023 in Abagatan ti Manila, Alfonso, Cavite.
observations.reason: However, the Municipality has yet to submit sufficient documents detailing the personnel's functions, responsibilities, and their actual accomplishment; collaboration with Legal Team and HRMO is ongoing.

observations.recommendation: Henceforth, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the HRMO, in close coordination with the Municipal Budget Officer and other municipal offices, to study the manpower or staffing needs of the Municipality and to ensure that hiring of JO workers is primarily based on the specific request of concerned Municipal Department Heads or officials who shall certify that his/her Office needs the services of a JO worker for an identified piece of work.
observations.managementAction: The HRMO in close coordination with the offices concerned will re-organize the manpower complement in coordination with the HRMO and the Budget Officer. Five redundant JO workers whose job descriptions overlapped with the functions of existing municipal employees were terminated. In addition, contracts have been revised to reflect each JO’s daily rate. The HRMO also ensures that the contract is reviewed daily and revised as necessary to reflect appropriate remuneration.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the: a. Bids and Awards Committee to include as a requirement from the suppliers the CPR from the BFAD of the drugs to be supplied by them and/or validate the CPR from the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) online verification portal, and attach the CPR or proof thereof to the Disbursement Voucher; and b. General Services Officer and/or the official responsible for accepting deliveries of procured consumable items to submit the AIR to the COA within 24 hours from their acceptance in compliance with the COA Circular Nos. 92-386.
observations.managementAction: Adhering with the recommendations, the BFAD certificate is always included as a requirement from the supplier. The GSO accordingly is ensuring that AIR is acknowledged by COA within the required 24 hours from acceptance. The GSO promptly executed the recommendation. A copy of the AIR concerning the procured medicines is submitted to COA.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the concerned officials to submit the complete list of beneficiaries, indicating therein their complete names and quantity of rice or food supplies actually received or a written explanation/justification why they could not be submitted, for the Audit Team’s validation. Henceforth, ensure that all disbursements for welfare goods are supported with complete distribution list to prove any goods for judicious distribution of the same to avoid possible suspension and/or disallowance in the audit.
observations.managementAction: The Management ensured that they obtained and kept proof of receipt for any goods for distribution. The Management furnished the Audit Team a copy of photos taken during distribution, together with the list of COVID-19 patients for the year 2022. Henceforth, they will ensure that all disbursements for welfare goods are supported with complete distribution lists.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the GAD Focal Person and GFPS Chairperson to ensure that all PPAs to be included in the GAD Plan and Budget of the Municipality have well-defined gender issues to be addressed pursuant to the provisions of PCW, DILG, DBM and NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.
observations.managementAction: The GAD Focal Person convened the council and ensured that PPAs contained only those programs and activities that address well-defined gender issues.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Budget Officer and LDRRMO to provide justification on why the above-mentioned disbursements, the purposes of which were not related to disaster risk reduction and management activities, were charged to the LDRRMF, otherwise revert the funds to the LDRRMF, subject to compliance with budgeting, accounting and auditing rules and regulations, to avoid audit suspension and/or disallowance. We also recommended that administrative and regular operating expenses and other regular operating disbursements of similar nature be paid out from the General Fund (GF) of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: The Management ensured that the reversion of the subject amount was made immediately from the unappropriated surplus of the GF to the Trust Fund. Administrative and regular operating expenses and other disbursements of similar nature were charged to the GF of the Municipality.

observations.recommendation: We recommended management to require Mr. Gaudencio J. dela Cruz to immediately deposit/ refund/ liquidation his cash shortage totaling P3,038,010.29. Failure of the immediate refund/deposit/liquidation of his accountability will result to the elevation of the matter to the Office of the Ombudsman, if warranted, for the proper filing of the appropriate charges for the acts committed in violation of Section 105(2) of P.D. 1445, and be held liable for the missing/misappropriated government funds in accordance with Section 340 of R.A. 7160.
observations.managementAction: Based on the recently transmitted Cash Examination Report, the cash shortage of the DO increased to P3,713,699.74. Outstanding cash advances from previous years were left unliquidated.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 4, Page 42, Part II of the report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended management to advise the Disbursing Officer to use the standard format of a Liquidation Report in liquidating his cash advances and to liquidate them within the legal deadline in accordance with the applicable rules and regulations before another cash advance could be granted to him. Any unexpended balance of the cash advance should be refunded and be issued with an Official Receipt as part of his supporting documents in the liquidation.

We also recommended management to stop the withdrawals for cash payments to suppliers, obligations, travels and other expenses.  Payments should be made thru checks as cited in the regulations, except for those petty expenses which may be paid thru petty cash fund.  All existing withdrawals should be liquidated and closed. Excess funds must be returned to each appropriate fund properly.

Payments for payrolls, wages and petty expenses should be withdrawn under the regular cash advances and special cash advances granted must be payments for the specified purpose only.

Strict compliance with the existing rules and regulations on cash advances is enjoined.
observations.managementAction: The DO failed to fully liquidate/refund his cash advances before another cash advance could be granted and within the legal deadline. The Municipal Treasurer also failed to monitor his cash advances.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 6, Page 48, Part II of the report.

observations.managementAction: Cashbooks of the DO was not updated and reconciled with the Accounting Office.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 5, Page 46, Part II of the report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management take action on the fourteen (14) prior-year projects that were still recorded under Construction-in-Progress account by updating their individual statuses and pursue their full implementation.  Moreover, reclassify and recognize the Depreciation Expense for the year and the previous years’ if any, for completed projects still lodged to the CIP account.
observations.managementAction: Management traced back the transactions related to the projects and verification of entries. Actual inspections of the projects were made. However, audit validation revealed that majority of the identified PPAs were still in the CIP account and no update was made regarding its implementation.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 7, Page 52, Part II of the report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the accountant reconcile the affected accounts, effect the necessary adjustments and cause the liquidations or return of borrowed funds. Attention should be strictly focused especially on the Trust Fund Cash account which should be kept intact because the outlay from these funds shall only be for their intended purpose and not to be subjected to temporary borrowings.
observations.managementAction: Ending balances appearing in the prepared Statement of Financial Position revealed that both accounts were not reconciled.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 11, Page 64, Part II of the report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended Management to trace the custody and history of records of the accounts Advances to Contractors, Prepaid Expenses and the Biological Assets of the LGU for the proper adjustment of the accounts, preparation of supporting schedules and determination of proper accountability for government funds and property.
observations.managementAction: Management traced back the transactions related to the projects and verification of entries. Actual inspections of the projects were made.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 12, Page 65, Part II of the report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management timely and completely submit to the Auditor copies of monthly trial balances, quarterly financial statements, monthly journals and complete disbursement vouchers and report on collections and deposits, in compliance with COA Circular No. 2009-006 and Section 111 of PD 1445. Furthermore, we recommended that the LCE place internal controls (ex. routing slip) on the processing of financial transactions passing through certifying and authorizing officials as well as  monitor and expedite the Municipal Treasurer’s Office submission of collection and disbursement vouchers to the Accounting Office’ which shall likewise submit to COA after recording/journalizing without delay. Otherwise, the penalty being imposed for non-submission shall be strictly enforced.
observations.managementAction: Municipal Accountant committed to strictly comply with the timely submission of all the accounting reports. However, still failed to timely and completely transmit financial accounts of the LGU.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 14, Page 71, Part II of the report.

observations.managementAction: Management failed to submit the approved time extensions of the subjected projects. Moreover, approved time extensions to CY 2021 PPAs by contract were not fully attached to the DVs.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 16, Page 79, Part II of the report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor create an Inventory Committee
composed of adequate number of members to be able to complete the physical
inventory of the PPE in three months or less, and who shall be temporarily
relieved of all their regular duties to devote their full time in the conduct of the
physical inventory taking until the same is completed, in compliance with COA
Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020. After which, it is recommended that
the Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Inventory Committee to prepare a Physical Inventory Plan and accordingly
conduct a comprehensive physical inventory of all PPE of the Municipality and
prepare the corresponding Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant
and Equipment (RPCPPE) following the guidelines set forth in said COA Circular;
b. General Services Officer to update the GSO’s property records based on the
RPCPPE; and
c. Municipal Accountant and the OIC, General Services Officer to analyze and
reconcile the discrepancy of P7,372,653.82 between the Accounting records
and RPCPPE and make the necessary adjustments.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the OIC-Municipal Agricultural
Officer and other concerned officials to submit to him, copy furnished the Audit
Team, a written report detailing their explanation on why the subject projects were
left idle for a significant period of time and the specific actions they plan to
undertake to ensure the immediate utilization of the animal dog cages and
slaughterhouse, otherwise relevant disbursements will be declared unnecessary
and thus will be disallowed in audit.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant
to make necessary adjusting entries to recognize the Slaughterhouse as asset in
the General Fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to
prepare a Journal Entry Voucher for the cancellation of the checks and
accordingly, for the restoration of cash equivalent of the stale checks and
recognition of the appropriate payable/liability accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC, General Services
Officer to prepare a Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued and submit it to
the Municipal Accountant who, on the basis thereof, shall properly record the
procurement or receipt of supplies and materials and in addition, adopt the
Perpetual Inventory System of accounting.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to see
to it that all transactions of the Municipality are properly recorded and the
accounts used are in accordance with COA Circular No. 2015-009.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct
the Municipal Accountant and Disbursing Officer to adhere strictly to the
provisions of COA Circular Nos. 97-002 and 2012-004 in the granting, utilization
and liquidation of cash advances.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal General Services
Officer to prepare the Property Inventory Form listing all the insurable properties
of the Municipality and submit the same to the Office of the Auditor and GIF, GSIS
not later than April 30 of each year, and insure subject assets with the GSIS in
compliance with the foregoing laws, rules and regulations.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendations that the Municipal Mayor
require the OIC-General Services Officer to:
a) exert effort to look for the relevant Deeds of Sale, Certificate of Registration
and other documents that will facilitate the transfer of the motor vehicles in
its name and the issuance of plates of heavy equipment;
b) follow-up with the LTO for the issuance of red plates to vehicles bearing
conduction sticker; and
c) expedite the registration of two motor vehicles with the Land
Transportation Office and their insurance coverage so that the Municipality
will be compensated in case of damage or loss.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC- General Services
Officer to prepare the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property
for the additional nine motor vehicles for disposal reported in 2021 and proceed in
accordance with existing guidelines with the disposition of the ten 10 motor
vehicles which have been inspected by the Audit Team.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to
return to the Bureau of Treasury the balances of the funds transfers from various
NGAs that had been dormant for more than five years or the purpose of which had
already been achieved by the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:
a. the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council (MDRRMC) to
identify and incorporate, under a separate caption in the LDRRMFIP, the
specific projects and activities to be charged against the unexpended
previous years’ LDRRMF;
b. all concerned officials to ensure that all projects and programs in the
LDRRMFIP are fully implemented.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and concerned Municipal officials
strictly comply with the directives or existing guidelines promulgated by the DBM
on disbursements of funds coming from the national government and with
specific purpose, otherwise any deviation therefrom will be suspended or
disallowed in audit.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Planning and
Development Officer, the Municipal Engineer, the Bids and Awards Committee and
other concerned officials to coordinate with each other to immediately determine
and resolve the cause of delays in the implementation of the three unimplemented
20 per cent Development Fund projects and henceforth, ensure that all projects
funded under said fund are implemented according to plan.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct all members of the GAD Focal
Point System to conduct a gender analysis of the Municipality’s programs and
projects through the administration of the HGDG for purposes of GAD budget
attribution.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Engineer and
other concerned officials to ensure that the required project signboards and/or
public notices are displayed at the project location and that a report of the
Municipal’s compliance with the posting requirement is submitted to the COA
Audit Team within ten (10) days after the award of the infrastructure project or
before the start of the activity in adherence to the provisions of COA Circular
No. 2013-004.

1.7	We recommended that Management strictly comply with the provisions of DBM-DOH Joint Circular Nos. 1 and 2, S. 2020 and Joint Circular No. 1, S. 2021 on the grant and payment of Special Risk Allowance (SRA) and Active Hazard Duty Pay (AHDP) to eligible Health Workers and other further issuances involving grant of additional benefits that are based on work attendance and/or output be substantiated with daily time records (DTRs)/logbook and/or proof of output.
1.8	The Management accepted the audit findings and committed to comply with audit recommendation that further issuances involving grant of benefits based on actual attendances/output shall be substantiated with documentary requirements that involve individual DTRs/accomplishment reports.

2.9	We recommended that the LGU management update their three (3) FDP Boards and constantly and timely submit the required documents in the FDP Portal pending the establishment of the live and GWHS-hosted LGU website. Moreover, comply on the posting of the monthly Report on Fund Utilization and Status of Implementation of BGCM PPAs in at least three (3) conspicuous public places in the locality.
2.10	The Management admitted the audit findings and has immediately updated the three (3) FDP Boards of the LGU. However, the management asserted that there were instances where some citizens detach copies on the board without the LGU's consent and sometimes were being vandalized. Nonetheless, the Management assured the team that constant updating of the FDP Boards shall be treated with highest degree of responsibility and accountability.

3.6	We recommended that the Management require:
All the accountable officers/collectors the full remittance of their collections daily. This will discourage possible personal use or misappropriation of the fund at their hands;
The Municipal Treasurer to set a cut-off time for the other collectors’ remittances to the LO (i.e. every 4:30 PM) and weekly deposit to the depository bank to ensure the full compliance with the regulations and to safeguard government funds; and
The Liquidating Officer to update her cash books daily and to fully deposit all her collections to the depository bank.
3.7	The Management committed to comply with the audit recommendations thru: (a) Daily remittance of collections to LO and to set-up daily cut-off of remittances; (b) Weekly deposit of total collections and remittances; and (c) Daily updating of cashbooks

4.13	We recommended that the LCE require the Disbursing Officer to immediately deposit/refund/liquidate his accounted cash shortage totaling P3,713,699.74. Failure of the immediate refund/deposit/liquidation of his accountability will result to the elevation of the matter to the Office of the Ombudsman, if warranted, for proper filing of appropriate charges for the acts committed in violation of Section 105(2) of PD No. 1445, and be held liable for the missing/misappropriated government funds in accordance with Section 340 of RA No. 7160.
4.14	We also recommended that the LCE relieve soonest Mr. Gaudencio dela Cruz as Disbursing Officer and appoint another bonded employee of the Treasury Office to undertake disbursing functions for other funds such as petty cash funds, advances for operating expenses, advances for payroll, travel expenditures, etc. in order to assist the current DO to be more focused in reconciling with the Accounting Office and in settling his accountabilities the soonest.
4.15	The management committed to resolve the issue on the shortage of the DO by designating temporarily a new accountable officer with disbursing function in order to give an ample time (until August 2022) to Mr. dela Cruz in settling and reconciling his unliquidated cash advances declared as cash shortage in the conducted cash examination.

5.8	The Management commented that for CY 2021 transactions, the Accounting Office and the DO reconciled the monthly disbursements per Cash DJ with the records of the DO. The unexpended balances for the months were returned and ORs were issued thru the use of Liquidation Reports. The Accounting Office, nevertheless, commits to religiously and immediately reconcile the accounting records and the cash books of the DO.

7.9	We recommended that Management take action on the 22 prior-year projects that were still recorded under Construction-in-Progress account by updating their individual statuses and pursue their full implementation.  Moreover, reclassify and recognize the Depreciation Expense for the year and the previous years’ if any, for completed projects still lodged to the CIP account. We propose the following entries to properly reflect the balance of the CIP, PPE and Depreciation Expense accounts:
7.10	The management expressed their commitment in updating the status of completion of the identified PPAS that comprise the CIP account. According to the letter dated April 7, 2022, by the Municipal Mayor, a partial reconciliation with the Engineering Office had already been made and revealed that some of those PPAs were already completed.

8.8	We recommended that:
The Municipal Accountant and the Inventory Committee on Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE) reconcile the above accounts and submit reports for validation;
The Municipal Accountant verify and book up any unrecorded PPE at historical cost together with its accumulated depreciation if records are available, if none, at appraised value or replacement cost; and
The Municipal Accountant submit Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) for the reconciliation/ adjustments made thereat.
8.9	During the exit conference, the management informed the team that in order to update the correct balances of the PPE of the LGU, an Inventory Committee was created and has already started to conduct the physical count of all PPEs of the LGU for the one-time cleansing of account balances. The management further committed to submit JEVs as a result of the inventory taking and reconciliation of records.

10.6	We recommended that Management require the GSO to submit a Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) to the Accounting Office for their basis in dropping subject inventories from their books and that the Municipal Accountant adjust the account to better reflect the amount in the financial statements and to recognize semi-expendable properties as Expense once issued.
10.7	In a letter dated April 7, 2022 of the Municipal Mayor, he explained that:
The expenses recorded as Other Supplies and Materials Inventory account will be scrutinized to determine if there are items that could meet the capitalization threshold of P15,000.00 and prepare the necessary adjusting entry.

11.5	We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reconcile the affected accounts, effect the necessary adjustments and cause the liquidations or return of borrowed funds. Attention should be strictly focused especially on the Trust Fund Cash account which should be kept intact because the outlay from these funds shall only be for their intended purpose and not to be subjected to temporary borrowings.
11.6	The Municipal Accountant expressed her commitment to reconcile the accounts and to prepare the necessary adjusting entries to conform with the guidelines.

12.6	In a letter dated April 7, 2022 of the Municipal Mayor, he explained that:
On the various accounts totaling P4,498,006.01 pertaining to receivable, prepayments and inventory accounts, the management commits to trace the custody and history of records for the preparation of adjustments and the related schedules.

14.12	We recommended that the LGU management thru the Municipal Accountant submit regularly to the Auditor complete copies of monthly trial balances, quarterly financial statements, monthly journals and complete disbursement vouchers and report on collections and deposits, in compliance with COA Circular No. 2009-006 and PD No. 1445. Furthermore, we recommended that the LCE place internal controls (ex. routing slip) on the processing of financial transactions in order to monitor and expedite the Accounting Office’ submission. Otherwise, the penalty being imposed for non-submission shall be strictly enforced.
14.13	In a letter of the Municipal Accountant dated February 18, 2022, she explained that:
The management commits to timely submit the copies of Monthly Trial Balance, Quarterly Financial Statements, Disbursement Vouchers and Report of Collection and Deposit in compliance to COA Circular No. 2009-006 and PD No. 1445.
14.14	Moreover, during the exit conference, monthly and quarterly reports were difficult to generate due to the use of a manual accounting system. The audit team, nonetheless, explained that application of an accounting software, specifically the e-NGAS, may be held in abeyance by reason of the new Government Accounting Manual to be followed by all the LGUs.

15.15	We recommended that the Local Chief Executive, together with the members of the Municipal Planning and Development Council:
Update the Annual Investment Plan through a revisit of the unimplemented development projects and review its viability and necessity, taking into consideration and observing the guidelines set in DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 01, series of 2020 dated November 4, 2020, as well as other budgeting guidelines; and
Include in the Annual Investment Plan only those projects which can be implemented during the year in order to maximize the utilization of the 20% DF.
15.16	During the exit conference, the management informed the Audit Team that most of the identified unimplemented projects refer to PPAs implemented by administration. Due to its nature of implementation, the process from procurement of direct materials to actual construction of the project entails more time as compared to projects implemented by contract. Nevertheless, the management exhibited their commitment in complying with the recommendations of the audit team by identifying unimplemented PPAs and reassessing its viability and necessity and to propose new PPAs or to augment existing PPAs that need additional funds in order to fully maximize the utilization of the 20% DF.

16.12	We recommended that the Management:
Require the concerned Project Engineers/Inspectors to immediately submit the Statement of Work Accomplished as of the date of the actual completion of the mentioned projects in Annex A, as basis in the computation of liquidated damages to be imposed and collected from the contractors.
For CY 2022 transactions, attach the approved extensions of contract time, if there is, with the Disbursement Vouchers to support payment;
For procurement of Goods, completely fill-out the important dates or periods in the POs, Bidding Documents, Delivery Receipts and IARs to be used as basis for the computation of any Liquidated Damages for delayed implementation; and
Prepare a GANTT Chart for close monitoring of the implementation of projects and advice the contractors to monitor also their compliance with their contracts to avoid delayed implementation of projects and adverse findings like liquidated damages.
Henceforth, use the date of receipt of NTP as the contract effectivity date pursuant to Section 37.4.1 of the same RIRR in the reporting and monitoring of projects.
Require the contractors to post Warranty Security to support the release of their final payment.
Attach in the current DV the photocopy/ies of the vouchers of all previous progress payments to show the amount of previous claims and aid in the computation of final payment.
For final payments, ensure that the actual date of completion appearing in the SWA coincide with the date indicated in the Certificate of Completion and/or Inspection Report.
16.13	In a letter of the Municipal Mayor dated April 7, 2022, he explained that:
The management ensures that liquidated damages have been imposed on all defaulting contractors/suppliers. The practice on the grant of additional seven (7) days to contractors/suppliers as their mobilization period resulted to the deficiency in computation of liquidated damages. Given that this should not be the practice, henceforth, the management will recognize the effectivity of contracts on the date of receipt of the Notice to Proceed.
The management commits to submit the Statement of Work Accomplished as of the date of actual completion of the projects mentioned… Also, the management commits to ensure that photocopy of previous payments and approved variations, if any, shall be attached in all the current DVs, Warranty Security shall be posted prior to issuance of final payment and that dates of Purchase Orders, Inspection and Acceptance Reports and other documents shall be filled out. GANTT chart will be prepared by the Engineering Office for the monitoring of the implementation of projects.

17.8	We recommended that:
Management intensify efforts to improve the utilization rate of the 5% MDRRMF as a whole and update the MDRRMC of the unimplemented PPAs in order to provide information to aid in the planning of the implementation or to the reprogramming of those PPAs deemed not anymore priority of the Municipality;
The Municipal Accountant to reclassify the DRRM funds received from national agencies to close the Due to NGAs and transfer the amount to Trust Liability-DRRMF;
The LDRRMO to faithfully submit on time the monthly Report on the Sources and Utilizations of LDRRMF using the standard format, duly prepared and certified correct by the Municipal Accountant, on or before the 15th day after the end of each month to the Office of the Auditor; and
Management strictly submit in full all financial transaction documents for each month in order to support reports that are relevant to audit and submit the lacking MDRRM accounts in order to validate the transferred STF.
17.9	In a letter of the Municipal Mayor dated April 4, 2022, he explained that:
…the implementation of the various PPAs under the 5% MDRRM fund is crucial especially during this time of the COVID-19 pandemic. The management commits to intensify efforts in the implementation of the different PPAs under the 5% MDRRM Fund since the year 2021, the LGU was able to utilize 62% of the available fund.
The additional funds from the national government amounting to P1,260,717.00 will be reclassified to the appropriate accounts, Trust Liability-DRRM. Admittedly, the accounting records for the MDRRM Fund shown in the Notes to the Financial Statements needs further reconciliation especially those appearing to be from the year 2017 and 2018. The preparation of the monthly utilization report which entails records from the LDRRMO and the Accounting Office could really help in monitoring the status of the various PPAs under the MDRRM Fund. Thus, the management commits to religiously submit the monthly report on the Sources and Utilization of the LDRRMF. Also, the accounting and treasury office shall ensure the submission of the entire financial transaction documents subject in this audit and that moving forward, all financial transaction documents shall be timely submitted.

18.11	We recommended that the Local Chief Executive, through the LSB, ensure that there is an approved budget for the SEF so that priority programs, projects and activities shall be implemented and that the target objectives of the national government during the time of pandemic are considered in the utilization of the fund.
18.12	The management admitted that the LSB failed to convene due to occurrence of COVID-19 cases. Nonetheless, the Management committed to adhere on the preparation of SEF Budget and its utilization, as well as the conduct of regular LSB meetings. For CY 2022, the LSB already conducted a meeting which tackled the preparation of SEF budget.

19.9	We recommended that:
Management thru the Municipal Treasurer formulate strategic collection schemes and active collection campaigns, like offering of increased discount percentages and/or condonation of penalty programs, so that the amount of P1,738,636.14 in the receivable accounts will decrease until such time that the amount recorded in the receivable accounts were only the uncollected RPT and SET for the current year;
The Management coordinate with the Punong Barangays with regard to the collection of RPT and SET from delinquent tax payers for easier dissemination of notices; and
19.10	In a letter of the Municipal Mayor dated March 3, 2022, he explained that:
The collection on Real Property Tax for the year 2021 pertains to current year and taxes collected for the prior years. A reconciliation of records will be made to reflect the correct amount of receivable for prior years. Further, the management commits to formulate a collection scheme and active collection campaigns in order to collect the long overdue taxes.

20.7	We recommended that Management continuously comply with the provisions of RA No. 9710 including its IRR, to fully adopt the DILG Gender and Development Plan and Budget Monitoring System (GAD-PBMS) and to submit the CY 2021 GAD Accomplishment Report for validation by the audit team.
20.8	The Management commented that the submitted CY 2021 GPB generated from the GAD-PBMS included double entries of PPAs. Nonetheless, the Management agreed with the audit recommendations by adopting the GAD-PBMS and the submission of the CY 2021 GAD AR.

21.7	We recommended that Management, henceforth, comply with the provisions of BIR Revenue Memorandum Order No. 23-2014 on the withholding of taxes to help the government in generating revenues to meet its appropriation requirement and to avoid penalties and legal action against the erring withholding agents from the BIR.
21.8	The Management admitted that they had overlooked that NUVELCO is no longer exempt from paying VAT, and commits to comply with the pertinent regulations on the collection of withholding taxes on payments to NUVELCO.

22.18	We recommended that Management instruct the personnel in-charge of the leave cards to adjust the balance of the vacation leave and sick leave of the concerned employee by retaining five (5) vacation leave credits and charging 59.456 against her sick leave credits. We also recommended that the erring employee return/refund the P60,738.44 to the Municipal Treasury Office for intentionally accepting her salary in error.
22.19	Lasty, we recommended that the HRMO exhaust first the accumulated leave credits of officers and employees before their application for leave without pay could be granted in consonance with the above-mentioned regulations. Application for leave without pay in excess of one (1) month should also be supported by a clearance signed by the head of the agency.
22.20	The audit team was informed that the recommended changes in the leave card of the personnel involved in the issue has been effected and that she had already settled to the LGU the amount she willfully received as salary in error. Furthermore, the management committed to comply with the audit recommendations on approval of leave credits without pay and clearance requirements.

23.11	We recommended that Management instruct the personnel in-charge of the leave cards to adjust the balance of the vacation leave and sick leave of the concerned employees by exhausting first their vacation leave credits before their sick leave credits may be monetized. Henceforth, strictly observe the provisions of Sections 22 and 23, Rule XVI of the Omnibus Rules on Leave and CSC Resolution No. 000034 on the granting and approval of monetization of leave credits.
23.12	We also recommended that Management ensure that payments of monetization for more than 30 days should be supported with valid and justifiable reason and necessary supporting documents as provided in COA Circular 2012-001.
23.13	The Management commented that the said monetization was given in consideration of the effect of the COVID-19 pandemic and that it would help in case they have contracted the virus. Nevertheless, the Management committed to adhere with pertinent guidelines on the granting and approval of monetization of leave credits.

24.7	We recommended that the LGU strictly follow the provisions of DBM BC 2021-3 in granting the Collective Negotiation Agreement incentive. Stop paying the CNA if the required minimum average 70% threshold is not met/achieved as this constitute irregular expenditure as defined in COA Circular No. 2012-003 and disallowable in audit.  The LGU management is encouraged to do their best in the implementation and accomplishments of its approved PPAs for the benefit of its constituents, and in order to also qualify and enjoy the incentive/reward given under the CNA.
24.8	The management commented that the total accomplishments of PPAs were based on financial and physical completion. For financial completion, the management asserted that the delayed payment on some PPAs was due to the surge of COVID-19 cases last August-September 2021, which prompted the LGU to impose localized lockdown. In view of the good recommendations of the audit team, the management commits to do its best in the implementation and accomplishments of its approved PPAs and to obey the annual guidelines of the DBM related to CNA Incentive.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and the OIC-General Services Officer to analyze and reconcile the discrepancy between the Accounting records and RPCPPE and make the necessary adjustments.
observations.managementAction: There was already an on-going analysis and reconciliation of PPE discrepancies between the GSO and the Accounting Office.
observations.reason: Analysis and reconciliation of PPE discrepancies between the GSO and the Accounting Office is still on-going.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer to prepare and submit to the Municipal Accountant a complete and updated list showing the names of taxpayers with taxes due and collectible based on the RPT Account Register/Taxpayer’s Index Cards to enable the latter to set up the receivables at the beginning of the year, to establish with certainty the reported RPT and SET Receivables as of year-end. We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
a) make necessary adjusting entry to reflect the correct balance of delinquent RPT and SET Receivables as of December 31, 2020; and
b) henceforth, ensure that the financial transactions and operations of the Municipality are recorded in conformity with generally accepted accounting principles and in accordance with pertinent laws and regulations.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer prepared and submitted a complete and updated certified list showing the names of taxpayers with the amount of taxes due and collectible based on the RPT Account Register/Taxpayer’s Index Cards. The corresponding adjusting entries were already prepared and recorded. The financial transactions and operations of the Municipality are recorded in conformity with generally accepted accounting principles and in accordance with pertinent laws and regulations.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that all transactions of the Municipality are properly recorded using the accounts prescribed under COA Circular No. 2015-009.
observations.managementAction: The expenses related to Welfare Goods distributed and food expenses for COVID Patients were charged to the Welfare Goods and Medical, Dental, and Laboratory Supplies Expense and the distributed/used portion to Welfare Goods Expense and Medical, Dental, and Laboratory Supplies Expense, respectively, which will be closed out to the Transfer from General Fund of Unspent DRRMF account at the end of the year for proper monitoring of receipts, issuances, and balances of goods procured out of the prior year’s unexpended balances of LDRRMF. The corresponding adjusting entries were already prepared and recorded.
observations.reason: The Municipal Accountant explained that they failed to coordinate with Budget Officer to change fund charging and to advise them to process necessary realignment if needed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Engineer and other concerned officials to properly record the purchases of welfare goods and medical supplies under the accounts prescribed under COA Circular No. 2015-009 and that the Municipal Accountant prepare and subsequently record corresponding adjusting entry to recognize in the General Fund the Medical Equipment worth P110,000.00, including the corresponding depreciation expense.
observations.managementAction: The corresponding adjusting entries were already prepared and recorded.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our previous year’s recommendations that the Municipal Mayor require the OIC-General Services Officer to:
a) exert effort to look for the relevant Deeds of Sale, Certificate of Registration and other documents that will facilitate the transfer of the motor vehicles in its name and the issuance of plates of heavy equipment;
b) follow-up with the LTO for the issuance of red plates to vehicles bearing conduction sticker; and
c) expedite the registration of two motor vehicles with the Land Transportation Office and their insurance coverage so that the Municipality will be compensated in case of damage or loss.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC-General Services Officer to prepare the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property for the additional nine motor vehicles for disposal reported in 2021 and proceed in accordance with existing guidelines with the disposition of the ten (10) motor vehicles which have been inspected by the Audit Team.
observations.managementAction:
In her letter dated June 1, 2022, the OIC – GSO replied that:
 the Municipality procured 12 new vehicles and they are still waiting for the issuance of the respective vehicles’ Red License Plates.
 they shall continue to register the vehicles identified with the Land Transportation Office and secure the needed insurance coverage.
 the Municipality, through respective committee on inventory and disposal, shall dispose 3 identified unserviceable vehicles by manner of donation.
 they shall continue to search relevant documents through the Inventory Committee created.
observations.reason: The Management are still looking for the relevant Deeds of Sale, Certificate of Registration and other documents that will facilitate the transfer of the motor vehicles in its name and the issuance of plates of heavy equipment; follow-up with the LTO for issuance of red plates; and ongoing processing for disposal and registration.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to require all Accountable Officers to adhere strictly to the provisions of COA Circular Nos. 97-002 and 2012-004 in the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances.
observations.managementAction: The Management also adhere and commit the immediate implementation of the recommendation and will establish an effective system to avoid non-compliance.
observations.reason: The Municipality explained that Landbank already approved salary automation; hence no cash advance will be required for payroll, and they are working to establish controls to avoid non-compliance.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Engineer and other concerned officials to ensure that all projects and activities in the 20 per cent Development Fund are implemented accordingly to ensure that the socio-economic, environmental, and other benefits intended to be derived from the projects are optimally enjoyed by the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: The Waterworks office is already in coordination with the Primewater Tagaytay for additional fund concerns and with supervision of Provincial Alfonso Government Office; the Municipality waited for the completion of the construction of deep well funded by the Provincial Government which will provide the definite water source.
observations.reason: The Municipality coordinated with Primewater Tagaytay and Provincial Government; construction and funding coordination caused delay in implementation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the Chairman of the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) submit to the Audit Team within 10 days from receipt hereof a written explanation why the amount of P3,050,000.00 or part thereof paid by the Municipality in CY 2020 should not be disallowed in audit notwithstanding the following: a) The procurement thereof was not in accordance with Annex H, Section V, D(7) of the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations (IRR) of Republic Act (RA) 9184; b) Several consultants performed duties which were not highly technical and/or could be performed by incumbent officials; c) The Municipality did not submit proof that its own officials and employees did not have the capability, capacity and/or time to undertake the subject services; d) payments totaling P1,070,000.00 were not supported with accomplishment reports, contrary to COA Circular 2012 – 001; and e) hiring of three lawyers and one law firm was without prior concurrence of the COA, in violation of COA Circular No. 95-011. We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to submit the accomplishment reports to substantiate the payments made totaling P1,070,000.00 and hereafter ensure that all accomplishment reports are properly documented and attached on the disbursement vouchers.
observations.managementAction: The Management submitted the work accomplishment reports to substantiate the payments totaling P1,070,000.00 and hereafter ensure that all accomplishment reports are properly documented and attached on the disbursement vouchers.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the: a) Municipal Budget Officer and Accountant to submit explanation/justification, and further evaluation of the expenses incurred for the landscaping of Bilog Elementary School and refund the honoraria and consultant fees they received from the Municipality, considering also that the observation had already been discussed with Management in prior year’s audit, otherwise the subject transactions will be disallowed in audit; and b) ALS Coordinators and District Supervisors to refund the honoraria and consultant fees they received from the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Budget Officer and Accountant explained that they charged the administrative expenses of the Alfonso District Office and the expenses incurred for the landscaping of Bilog Elementary School to the SEF, considering also that the observation had already been discussed with Management in prior year’s audit; they further explained that they presumed that the beautification project can be in support of Brigada Eskwela. The Municipal Accountant set up Accounts Receivable amounting to P25,500.00 for the refund of the honoraria. Of the P85,500.00 consultant fees and honoraria, P60,000.00 was already collected.
observations.reason: Accounts Receivable setup was done for refund of honoraria; of the P85,500.00 consultant fees and honoraria, P60,000.00 was collected and P25,500.00 remained uncollected as of December 31, 2021.

observations.recommendation:
We commended Management for its full compliance on the receipt and timely distribution of the subsidies to the identified eligible families within the Municipality. However, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the MSWDO to:
a. Submit evidence to account the refund made by three beneficiaries; 
b. Investigate further and provide proof to substantially contradict the allegations of six beneficiaries that they did not receive the full amount of P6,500.00; and 
c. Coordinate with concerned Barangay Officials to facilitate the submission to the COA of the updated contact numbers of beneficiaries, for the Audit Team’s further or expanded confirmation.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Management submitted the SAP Disqualification and Assistance Transfer Report and submitted the photos during the distribution of SAP Assistance showing the amount they received. The Municipal Management also submitted certifications from 32 Punong Barangays on the updated contact numbers of beneficiaries.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Bids and Awards Committee to explain/justify the discrepancies between the prices of the basic commodities procured by the City in response to the COVID-19 and their suggested retail prices issued by the authorized government agencies during the price freeze implementation, to prevent suspension and/or disallowance in audit.
observations.managementAction: The BAC Secretary justified that due to the urgency and skeletal work assignment, the procurement team overlooked the procedure and has to process the payment right away as some suppliers demanded on the previous transactions prior to the next delivery. The BAC also explained that there was difficulty in coming up with the required volume since there was also scarcity of rice and basic necessities in the market; that the supplier had to source additional manpower to pack and ship the goods to shorten the delivery time.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the BAC Secretariat to submit to the Audit Team the justifications on its inability to comply with the posting requirement of GPPB Circular No. 01-2020, for further evaluation.
observations.managementAction: The BAC Secretary justified that due to the urgency and skeletal work assignment, the procurement team overlooked the procedure and has to process the payment right away as some suppliers demanded on the previous transactions prior to the next delivery.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the General Services Officer to submit to the Auditor the required samples or photographs and the amended Acceptance and Inspection Reports indicating the complete specifications and brand names of the delivered PPEs to facilitate price verification and technical review of the reasonableness of the contract costs.
observations.managementAction: The General Services Officer submitted the required photographs and amended Acceptance and Inspection Reports indicating the specifications and brand names.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit the disbursement vouchers and its supporting documents to the Commission on Audit to avoid suspension/disallowance in audit and ensure that all accounts are submitted within the first ten days of the ensuing month as required under Section 7.2 of the Commission on Audit RRSA.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant submitted the disbursement vouchers and its supporting documents to the Commission on Audit to ensure timely audit and compliance.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare an adjusting journal entry to take up the unutilized balance of the BGCM by debiting Prior Year Adjustments and crediting the Due to NGAS account. Henceforth, succeeding receipts of the fund, if any, shall be recorded as liability under Due to NGAs in conformity with GAM for LGU.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant had already adjusted the balance of BGCM to Due to NGAs.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Management to:

a.	Maintain a Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed and Balances which is sufficiently corroborated with acknowledgement receipt of donations, duly acknowledged distribution lists and inventory of remaining undistributed items. The resulting report should be submitted to the Office of the Auditor within the period prescribed under COA Circular 2020-009;
b.	Advise the Accounting Office to make the necessary adjusting entry to recognize the noted undistributed rice goods; and
c.	Inform the Accounting Office and the GSO of donations in-kind received by the LGU to facilitate the monitoring of goods and its recognition in the books of the LGU for CY 2021.
observations.managementAction: The MSWDO are maintaining now a Summary of all Donations Received. The GSO are inspecting all the donations received. The Accounting Office are also informed for proper recording.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management explain/justify their purchase of the COVID-19 testing kits from suppliers without FDA authorizations to avoid the suspension of the transaction. Moreover, we recommended that management directly procure COVID-19 testing kits to suppliers who are authorized by the FDA to ensure the provision of accurate and reliable information regarding results of COVID-19 tests.
observations.managementAction: Submitted justification and copy of FDA Authorization for the supplier of the COVID-19 testing kits. Management also procured FDA approved COVID-19 testing kits from FDA authorized Distributor for CY 2021.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Accounting Office to rectify the accounts used in recording the transaction through a correcting entry. Necessary adjustment should be made to record the effect of liquidation documents submitted thereafter. We further recommended the Management to advise the concerned Implementing Agency on the submission of utilization report / liquidation documents for proper validation, verification of the Audit Team, and for appropriate adjustment by the Accounting Office.
observations.managementAction: Requested liquidation report from the League of Municipalities of the Philippines-Nueva Vizcaya Chapter.
observations.reason: No adjustment was made to rectify the accounts used in recording the transaction through a correcting entry. For further validation and for possible suspension, if management fails to submit LR.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the management:

Require the requisitioning office to always be specific in describing the items they are about to purchase and to carefully assess the quantity of items based on their needs;
Submit the RDS of the missing 6,458 pieces of washable face masks; and
Adhere to the guidelines and updates set by the DOH and other government agencies in battling COVID-19.
observations.managementAction: The management submitted the RDS for the 6,458 distributed washable masks. Management ensured that subsequent purchase requests shall contain specific and detailed description.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management submit the lacking documentary requirements on the procurement of the sanitation tents to establish the legality and capability of the supplier and to update the GSO and Accounting records as to who acknowledged the unaccounted nine (9) units of open tents. Failure to immediately submit the lacking requirements shall lead to the issuance of Notice of Suspension (NS) and subsequent failure to settle this NS shall further lead to the disallowance of the transaction for being irregular in audit.
observations.managementAction: The management submitted copy of the Acknowledgement Receipt for the 9 (nine) units of open tents. Submitted justification and copy of the lacking requirements.
Management submitted the additional Relief Distribution Sheets (RDS). The Accounting Office were required to post audit all RDS whenever there is a distribution

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the management update the three (3) FDP Boards of the LGU and constantly and timely submit the required documents in the FDP Portal pending the establishment of the LGU’s internet domain. Moreover, comply on the posting of the monthly Report on Fund Utilization and Status of Implementation of BGCM PPAs in at least three (3) conspicuous public places in the locality.
observations.managementAction: The management continuously updated the LGU account in the FDP Portal, however, the three (3) FDP boards of the LGU were rarely updated. Moreover, the management continued to comply on the posting requirements of the monthly Utilization Status of the BGCM Fund.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 2, Page 37, Part II of the report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management require the Liquidating Officer to fully deposit all her collections to the depository bank and install a system of internal control in the Municipal Treasury Office that will ensure that issuance of official receipts is supported by actual receipt of collections.
observations.managementAction: Based on the recent CER, some of the collections were still not deposited intact even if already remitted to the LO. The deferred collection from the Mun. Treasurer was already deposited.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 3, Page 41, Part II of the report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the increase of the fidelity bond of the Disbursing Officer who is also designated as Trust Fund Liquidating Officer in accordance with the Bureau of Treasury Circular No. 2-99 dated August 6, 2009 since his average total cash handled for the latest six (6) months of P9,578,527.00  is way beyond his approved maximum accountability of only P750,000.00.
We also recommended management to designate other personnel of the Treasury Office to fully handle the collections on the Trust Fund in order to reduce the Disbursing Officer’s cash accountability and in order for him to focus more on his function.
observations.managementAction: The DO's maximum cash accountability was increased to P5 million. Likewise, the DO is no longer Liquidating Officer in charge of the Trust Fund collections.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the management set a fund for the payment of the unsettled past due insurance premium of three heavy equipment with the GSIS as a requisite for its renewal in 2021 and to avoid the consequences of being at default with the agreement entered with the DBP.
observations.managementAction: The management set up a fund for the unsettled GSIS insurance for the three heavy equipment.

The management refrained from granting cash advances to elective officials with purposes other than their traveling expenses. Management also had initiated request for the write-off of long overdue cash advances.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 13, Page 68, Part II of the report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management enforce immediate settlement of the disallowances and comply with Section 7.1.1 of the Revised Rules and Regulations on Settlement of Accounts as implemented under COA Circular No. 2009-006. Moreover, we recommended that payments made by liable persons be presented to the Audit Team by the Municipal Treasurer in order to provide them with Notice of Settlements and subsequent removal of their disallowances in the COA record.
observations.managementAction: The Management still lacked enough enforcement of immediate settlement of disallowances. Moreover, full/partial settlement of disallowances were not being informed to COA for subsequent issuance of Notice of Settlement.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Finding No. 25, Page 113, Part II of the report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that:

The BAC-TWG carefully and sufficiently examine, validate and verify the eligibility, technical capability and financial requirements of winning bidders necessary for post-qualification and to coordinate with the Engineering Office and the Accounting Office on the status of the previously awarded contracts to the winning bidder;
The Engineering Office conduct an inspection as at the target completion date of the contract in order to serve as basis in computing liquidated damages and negative slippages, and to adopt the calibrated actions in response to delays in the implementation of infrastructure projects as per the GPPB Circular stated above; and
The management require the Engineering Office an update on the construction of the two (2) projects with known negative slippages and to conduct an investigation on why did the AMP Construction abandon the construction of the school building in Cawayan Elementary School and settle the issue first before new contract could be awarded.
observations.managementAction: The BAC ensured the adherence to the provisions of RA No. 9184 and GPPB Circular No. 03-2019. The Engineering Office ensured to closely monitor all on-going projects.
observations.reason: Management failed to conduct an investigation on why did the AMP Construction abandon the construction of the school building in Cawayan Elementary School and settle the issue first before new contract could be awarded.

observations.managementAction: The management adhered to the provisions of RA No. 9184 on the withholding of Retention Money and the issuance of Certificate of Final Acceptance. Management ensured that Certificates of Final Acceptance will only be issued after the lapse of the Defects Liability period. Further, contractors posted Warranty Securities for the completed project.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the requisitioning office to indicate the description/technical specifications of items to be procured in the Purchase Request, Abstract of Quotations and other documentary requirements in order to apprise the approving authorities and the suppliers of the items needed by the end user. Moreover, we recommended the Bids and Awards Committee to monitor all the purchase requests and notify the requisitioning office immediately for compliance of the lacking description/specification before processing the request.
observations.managementAction: The management ensured that all PRs contain a complete description/technical specification.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the management require the end user or the requisitioning office to fully describe or indicate the important details in the Technical Specifications of goods to be procured that is commensurate to the purpose to which those good are to be used. Moreover, submit an explanation as to why the management still pursued the transaction despite the bidder’s failure to deliver the operational truck that was subjected for the bidding.
observations.managementAction: The management required the requisitioning offices to fully describe or indicate the important details in the Technical Specifications of goods to be procured that is commensurate to the purpose to which those good are to be used.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the: a) Municipal Budget Officer and Accountant to submit explanation/justification, for further evaluation of the Audit Team, on why the administrative expenses of the Alfonso District Office and the expenses incurred for the landscaping of Bilog Elementary School were charged to the SEF, otherwise the subject transactions will be disallowed in audit; and b) Bids and Awards Committee to submit explanation/justification for procurements done for simultaneous cluster meet. We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure that the SEF is only utilized for purposes provided under Section 272 of RA No. 7160.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant and Municipal Budget Officer explained that upon receipt of the AOM, they immediately implemented the recommendation starting August 2020. For the beautification project, the LGU stated that they presumed that this project can be in support of Brigada Eskwela. The Management assured that no further allocation from the SEF will be made for projects of similar nature. The honoraria budgeted for the year for ALS Coordinators and District Supervisor will remain on hold. The Municipal Accountant further informed us that receivable accounts were already established for the refund of the said fees.
observations.reason: Several expenses not chargeable against SEF were still noted.

the OIC-General Services Officer to analyze and reconcile the discrepancy of
P1,613,154.31 between the Accounting records and RPCPPE and make the
necessary adjustments.

based on the RPT Account Register/Taxpayer’s Index Cards to enable the latter to

to:

and SET Receivables as of December 31, 2020; and
